text: A thirty year old female was found to have pancreatic SOL during gynecological evaluation. Her CT revealed an eight by ten centimeter solid cystic neoplasm of the pancreas abutting the spleen and involving the splenic vessels. She was therefore taken up for a laparoscopic distal pancreatectomy and splenectomy.
The surgical setup includes a 12mm right-hand working port for stapler introduction, with an optional epigastric port used for stomach retraction.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac using a harmonic device, preserving the gastrovibrioic arcade while dividing the short gastric vessels. Care is taken to gently divide the uppermost short gastric vessels to prevent bleeding. The posterior gastric artery, branching from the splenic artery and entering the stomach, is also divided to separate the stomach from the upper pole of the spleen.

The surgeon opens the lesser sac to the right, revealing approximately three to four centimeters of the pancreas beyond the tumor for further dissection. The stomach is retracted by hitching it up to the anterior abdominal wall using a stitch, providing clear visibility and access to the surgical field.

The surgeon begins the pancreatic dissection by locating the splenic artery at the upper border of the pancreas. The peritoneum is opened, and fat is dissected away to identify the artery by its pulsations. The artery is carefully controlled and slung, avoiding injury to the surrounding vasa vasorum to prevent bleeding. A substantial length of the artery is dissected free using gentle traction, and it is controlled with styes or hemologs.

During the dissection of loose areolar tissue, the surgeon identifies a polar branch to the spleen arising from the proximal pancreatic stump and a small pancreatic branch from the splenic artery entering the specimen. These arteries are carefully identified and divided.

The surgeon divides the remaining peritoneum at the upper border of the pancreas, carefully avoiding injury to the left gastric vessels. Subsequently, the splenic flexure of the colon is mobilized to improve access to the distal body and tail of the pancreas. The peritoneum on the lower border of the pancreas is opened, providing access to the retropancreatic planes.

The surgeon develops the avascular retropancreatic plane using blunt dissection and occasional harmonic device application. The pancreas and splenic vein are lifted anteriorly, while posteriorly, the kidney, renal vein, and aorta are visible, bound by the geratous fascia and its medial extensions.
The surgeon carefully dissects the engorged splenic vein, creating adequate retro-pancreatic space to prevent bleeding. The vein is slung, clipped, and divided using ties or hemologs.
The surgeon separates the pancreas from the genital fascia by dissecting the loose areolar tissue laterally, progressing towards the lino renal ligament.

The surgeon performs pancreatic transduction by creating a plane across the full thickness of the pancreas, starting from the lower border and extending to the upper border, excluding vessels. An umbilical tape is placed across the pancreas, which is lifted to widen the retropancreatic plane using sharp and blunt dissection. This creates a window large enough for the introduction of the endogia stapler, ensuring that proximal vascular stumps are not in the staple line.

The surgeon identifies the point of parenchymal transection in the pancreas and creates an adequate retroperitoneal tunnel for the introduction of the endo-GIA stapler. A small feeder branch from the left colic vessels to the tumor is divided. The lower part of the pancreas and tumor are separated from the mesocolon, and the lino renal ligament is lifted and divided, revealing its avascular nature.

The surgeon leaves some fat on the spleen to facilitate lifting it up, preventing splenic tears and maintaining a clear surgical field. Simple upward traction on the pancreatic tissue lifts the pancreas-spleen complex, allowing easy division of the lino renal ligament.

The surgeon develops a white pancreatic tunnel using a combination of blunt and sharp dissection, creating an adequate retropancreatic window for stapler introduction. The upper border of the tumor and the upper pole of the spleen are freed from the retroperitoneum and the left crus, completing the mobilization. The final step is the pancreatic transection, performed with a stapler for thin pancreases, or manually for thicker pancreases.

The surgeon uses an endo-GIA stapler with a gold reload due to the thickness of the pancreas, maintaining compression for three minutes to ensure hemostasis. A small sliver of pancreatic tissue not transected by the stapler is clipped and divided. The remaining retroperitoneal tissue tethering the tumor is dissected to complete the resection.

The resected specimen is placed into a specimen bag and pushed down into the pelvis for retrieval through a small incision. Retroperitoneal lavage is performed, and hemostasis is secured. The surgeon checks the vascular stumps and staple line for bleeding by increasing the blood pressure. Fibrin glue or Nectacryl glue is applied to the pancreatic staple line to aid hemostasis, and a drain is placed in the left upper quadrant.
